db_hits.text: Liber Logaeth ('Book of the Speech from God') is a manuscript compiled by John Dee from material received during the 1583 angel conversations with Edward Kelley, consisting of vast grids of letters and numbers arranged in tables. Dee understood the Liber Logaeth as a divinely transmitted text encoding the original language of creation — the speech through which God called the world into being. The manuscript represents the most cryptic and least deciphered component of the Enochian corpus, standing alongside the *Claves Angelicae* and the *Heptarchia Mystica* as a pillar of Dee's angelic system.

Ars Notoria is an Actor Term for a medieval ritual art of learning, memory, and intellectual preparation that emerged in Latin manuscript culture and became one of the most influential and controversial ritual texts of the later Middle Ages. Its earliest secure form belongs to the thirteenth-century Latin tradition, where prayers, notae, angelic names, and structured sequences of recitation are presented as aids to rapid mastery of the liberal arts and theology. Modern scholars such as Richard Kieckhefer, Claire Fanger, Frank Klaassen, and Robert Mathiesen have treated the work as a crucial witness to the intersection of devotion, education, and ritual technique rather than as a simple "spell book."

Historical Usage

In medieval usage the Ars Notoria belonged to the broad world of learned ritual, but it did not circulate as a single, fixed book. Manuscripts preserve multiple recensions, rewritten openings, and different arrangements of prayers and figures, suggesting that the text lived as a flexible ritual dossier rather than as a canonical treatise. Its core claim is that learning may be accelerated through disciplined prayer and exact attention to divine or angelic mediation. That claim sits near a number of other medieval textual forms, including confessionally framed devotional aids, angelic invocation, and occultized mnemonic practices. The work is also closely related to the wider Solomonic textual environment, not because Solomon is its only authority, but because the cultural authority of ancient wisdom and revealed knowledge gives the text its force. In later medieval and early modern manuscript culture, the Ars Notoria was often copied beside other ritual, astronomical, and devotional materials, a setting that reinforces its status as a manuscript practice rather than a single bounded genre.

Its historical importance lies partly in the way it destabilizes modern expectations. The text is neither a scholastic textbook nor an overtly demonic grimoire. It promises learning through ritual exactness, and in doing so it occupies the same broad zone that scholars such as Sophie Page and Frank Klaassen identify as the manuscript-based borderland between devotion, experiment, and illicit arts. The Ars Notoria therefore helps explain why medieval debates over magic cannot be reduced to a simple opposition between religion and superstition. It is better understood as a ritualized claim about the permeability of intellectual authority, where knowledge is something that may be petitioned, received, and disciplined rather than merely studied.

Scholarly Significance

Modern scholarship has made the Ars Notoria central to the study of medieval ritual learning. Kieckhefer placed the text within the larger history of illicit learned magic and manuscript ritual, showing that it belongs to a clerical culture in which learning, prayer, and suspect technique often coexisted. Claire Fanger has emphasized the role of ritual discipline, spiritual aspiration, and textual rewriting, while Klaassen has highlighted the manuscript transformations that carry the work across the later Middle Ages and Renaissance. Mathiesen's work is important because it points toward the ritual and Solomonic manuscript traditions in which the Ars Notoria circulates. Together these scholars make it possible to see the text not as an oddity on the edge of medieval learning, but as a revealing case study in the social life of ritual knowledge.

The term also matters because it helps define the portal's actor/analyst distinction. The Ars Notoria is not a medieval self-description for all learned magic, nor is it identical to modern categories such as "grimoire" or "occultism." It is one historical form within a much larger and unstable field. In that sense it is useful for tracing the historian's own categories: what counts as learning, what counts as ritual, and where the boundary lies between legitimate study and forbidden practice. The text sits beside notae, angelic invocation, ritual diagrams, and Solomonic magic in a way that exposes the manuscript ecology of medieval learned magic.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The Ars Notoria survives in Latin manuscript recensions with shifting titles, prefatory material, and explanatory additions. Some copies preserve a strong prayer-centered form, while others expand the ritual apparatus or emphasize visual and mnemonic features. The work's title itself is unstable, appearing in different manuscript families with minor variation, and its ritual authority is often strengthened by adjacent attributions to ancient or biblical wisdom. Because of that manuscript variability, any modern edition must be treated as one scholarly reconstruction among others rather than as the text's final medieval form. The work's transmission also overlaps with other ritual-learning traditions, especially texts associated with Solomon, angelic invocations, and the broader culture of memory arts.

Related Concepts

The Ars Notoria is inseparable from notae, since the visual figures are not ornamental but part of the ritual's logic. It also belongs to learned magic, although the modern category should not be collapsed into the text's own medieval world. Its proximity to pseudepigraphy and secret names shows how authority is staged through attributed antiquity and exact verbal transmission. Finally, the text's circulation beside ritual purity and angelic invocation reveals how deeply it depends on moral and devotional framing.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Learned Magic is an Analyst Term used by modern historians to describe textually transmitted magical practices cultivated by literate actors, often clerical, monastic, university, or courtly in training. It is one of the most useful categories in medieval magic scholarship because it highlights the social and manuscript conditions under which ritual texts, image magic, spirit conjurations, and related operations were copied and theorized. At the same time, it is not a medieval self-description.

Historical Usage

No medieval author sat down to write a unified treatise on learned magic in the sense modern scholarship intends. Instead we find specific arts and books: Ars Notoria, Liber Juratus, the Picatrix, image-magic treatises, necromantic compilations, angelic books, and various experimenta embedded in miscellanies. What allows these to be studied together is not a shared medieval title page but the recurring presence of literate procedure, Latin textuality, book circulation, and institutional learning.

Many such works borrow from the habits of prayer, liturgy, scholastic organization, and manuscript study even when they pursue goals condemned by theologians or canonists. The category therefore names a social and textual formation more than a single doctrinal content.

Scholarly Significance

Richard Kieckhefer, Claire Fanger, Frank Klaassen, Sophie Page, and Benedek Lang have each helped define the category by emphasizing different dimensions of it. Kieckhefer showed the clerical and manuscript milieu in which learned ritual could flourish. Fanger illuminated visionary and devotional logics. Klaassen made transformation across transmission central. Page stressed codicological and institutional settings, while Lang highlighted library preservation and ownership history.

The category is also historiographically important because it resists two distortions at once. Against sensationalism, it shows that ritual magic was often highly literate and intellectually structured. Against overcorrection, it insists that such texts were not merely harmless curiosities of book culture. Learned magic names a field in which textual expertise and suspect efficacy were historically intertwined.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The phrase itself is modern, but the archive it names is multilingual and materially diverse. Latin dominates much of the surviving western evidence, yet the field depends on Arabic, Hebrew, Greek, and vernacular transmissions as well. It includes stable-seeming books and unstable excerpts, elaborate diagrams and sparse rubrics, scholastic treatises and practical compilations. The category therefore requires relational browsing. No single grimoire or scholar can exhaust it.

Related Concepts

Learned magic overlaps with necromancy, but the latter captures only one part of the archive. It also intersects with astral image magic and angelic invocation, which represent major internal streams of the field. Finally, it is inseparable from manuscript miscellany and the clerical underworld, because the category is fundamentally about textual environments and social worlds.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Clerical Underworld is an Analyst Term associated especially with Richard Kieckhefer's description of literate clerics who copied, adapted, and sometimes used illicit ritual texts while remaining within ecclesiastical culture. The phrase is historiographical, not medieval self-description. It names a social formation rather than a doctrine: the paradoxical fact that the people most capable of producing complex magical manuscripts often belonged to the same clerical world that condemned them.

Historical Usage

Because the phrase is modern, its "historical usage" lies in the medieval evidence it seeks to organize. That evidence includes necromantic manuals in Latin, ritual books dependent on liturgical literacy, manuscript compilations copied by educated hands, and pastoral or theological sources that presuppose clerical familiarity with suspect practices. The term is useful precisely because it gathers these signs into a social hypothesis. It proposes that learned magic was not wholly external to ecclesiastical culture but parasitic on its literacies, rituals, and educational structures.

Scholarly Significance

The importance of the category is explanatory. It helps scholars move beyond the idea that magical texts were either wholly marginal or straightforwardly official. Instead, the clerical underworld identifies a zone of overlap in which clerics, students, and other literate actors could inhabit both conformity and transgression. This has been foundational for the interpretation of necromancy, notory prayer, and spirit-oriented ritual books. It also supports the portal's insistence that literacy, manuscript survival, and institutional critique belong together.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The archive behind the term is manuscript-based and dispersed. It includes books copied in clerical hands, texts saturated with liturgical language, and evidence of circulation in environments adjacent to schools, monasteries, and courts. Because the category is social and historiographical, it does not have one stable textual form. Its force comes from repeated patterns across different kinds of evidence. That makes it a relational concept almost by definition.

Related Concepts

The clerical underworld is tightly linked to learned magic and necromancy, since those are two of the fields where the pattern is most visible. It also bears on confession and pastoral care and magic, because clerical culture produced both the suspect manuscripts and many of the tools used to judge them.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Necromancy is an Analyst Term widely used in modern scholarship for learned ritual practices involving spirit conjuration, coercive prayer, circles, names, images, experimenta, and other textual procedures usually associated with literate or clerical environments. Medieval Latin sources often prefer nigromantia, whose historical range is broader and less stable than the modern English word. The portal therefore treats necromancy as a retrospective but indispensable category whose usefulness depends on continual comparison with medieval naming, manuscript context, and theological interpretation.

Historical Usage

Medieval evidence for necromancy survives in several registers at once: ritual manuscripts, scholastic condemnations, confessional manuals, polemical lists of forbidden arts, university statutes, and occasional legal accusations. These registers are not interchangeable. A manual may preserve conjurations, suffumigations, circles, and spirit names as technical procedure, while a pastoral writer recasts the same field as superstition or diabolic commerce. Later judges or demonologists may then absorb diverse ritual acts into a larger narrative of compact with demons. The category only becomes historically useful when these layers remain visible instead of being flattened into a single story.

The actor term nigromantia is especially important because it often names a broad field of illicit learned operations rather than literal traffic with the dead. The etymology suggests necro-communication, but medieval use can include invocation, image work, astral timing, adjuration, and spirit management more generally. That mismatch between etymology and practice is one reason the modern historian needs both caution and precision. "Necromancy" is often serviceable, but only if one remembers that the medieval archive was not organized by modern dictionary expectations.

Clerical and Learned Milieus

One of the category's most important historiographical functions is to keep the learned and clerical milieu of illicit ritual in view. Medieval necromantic texts are frequently copied in Latin by people trained in prayer, liturgy, books, and formal procedure. They may borrow the cadence of orations, the sequencing of ritual preparation, and the logic of textual exactitude from the same culture that condemns them. Necromancy is therefore not an external intrusion into learned life but one of the contested uses to which learned habits could be put.

This is why the category belongs so strongly to manuscript history. Necromantic operations are often difficult, heavily structured, and dependent on correct copying. They ask for names, diagrams, times, materials, and constrained performance. Their survival tells us not only that illicit ritual existed, but that it depended on environments capable of preserving and recombining complex written instructions.

Scholarly Significance

Necromancy matters because it is one of the strongest cases for studying magic through manuscripts rather than only through accusation. Richard Kieckhefer's Forbidden Rites remains central because it demonstrates how a necromantic archive could exist in a highly literate clerical milieu. Frank Klaassen extended that insight by emphasizing transformation across copying, compilation, and use, while Claire Fanger's work helped situate spirit-oriented ritual within a broader field of learned and visionary practices. Together these scholars turned necromancy from a sensational label into a serious codicological and intellectual-historical object.

The category is also important because it exposes the inadequacy of simple oppositions between religion and magic. Many necromantic materials borrow prayers, invocations, ritual purifications, and liturgical forms from the same Christian culture that denounces them. What is at stake is not simply content but authorization, intention, and relation to spirits. Necromancy is therefore a privileged site for studying medieval arguments over ritual legitimacy.

Ritual Technique and Textual Form

The internal techniques associated with necromancy vary widely. Some texts aim at knowledge, treasure, invisibility, erotic compulsion, or coercive domination; others aim to summon, bind, inspect, or dismiss spirits. Some rely more heavily on names, seals, and circles; others combine material procedures with prayers and suffumigations. There is no single canonical operation that stands for the whole field. What gives the archive coherence is less one technique than a recurring family resemblance: literate ritual, exacting performance, spirit-oriented efficacy, and uneasy theological status.

That family resemblance also helps explain the porous boundaries between necromancy and neighboring categories. A manuscript may blend invocations with image recipes, divinatory techniques, or devotional elements. Learned magic in practice was rarely sorted into perfectly discrete boxes, and necromancy must be read within that wider ecology rather than as a sealed subdiscipline.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Necromantic materials appear as discrete conjuration books, sections within miscellanies, spirit catalogues, experimenta, and ritual fragments. There is no single manuscript that can represent the field in full. This multiplicity is central to the portal's account of learned magic as a manuscript ecology rather than a tidy canon. The category therefore depends on families of evidence rather than on one masterpiece.

Its transmission also reveals strong overlap with scholastic, liturgical, and scientific book culture. The same habits that made the copying of sermons, computistical works, or theological distinctions possible also made the preservation of illicit ritual instructions possible. That overlap explains why expressions such as the "clerical underworld" have proven so durable in modern scholarship: they name not a hidden sect but a recurrent social location for textual experimentation.

Related Concepts

Necromancy is closely related to nigromantia, but the two should not simply be equated without comment. It overlaps strongly with learned magic and the clerical underworld because surviving evidence is so often textual and clerical. It also belongs with demonology, demonic pact, and suffumigation, which frequently supply the theological interpretation or practical mechanism of spirit-oriented ritual.

Literature

Bailey, Michael D. Magic and Superstition in Europe: A Concise History from Antiquity to the Present. Lanham: Rowman & Littlefield, 2007.
Fanger, Claire, ed. Conjuring Spirits: Texts and Traditions of Medieval Ritual Magic. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 1998.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 1998.
Klaassen, Frank. The Transformations of Magic: Illicit Learned Magic in the Later Middle Ages and Renaissance. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2013.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.

db_hits.text:
Divination is a Hybrid category in MedievalMagicDB: medieval actors used specific terms for particular techniques and condemnatory classes of knowledge, while modern scholars also employ "divination" as a broad analyst term for attempts to obtain hidden information through signs, lots, celestial judgment, bodily marks, dreams, spirits, or other mediated procedures. Because it gathers heterogeneous practices, the category must always be handled with care. Its usefulness lies in comparison and navigation, not in forcing all such practices into a single intellectual mold.

Historical Usage

Medieval sources discuss divination through a whole family of named arts: geomancy, chiromancy, hydromancy, sortilege, astrology, dream interpretation, lot casting, physiognomic reading, and spirit-mediated revelation, among others. Some are presented as observational or natural, others as unlawful or demonic, and many shift status depending on who is speaking. A university master, a confessor, a physician, and a compiler of practical recipes may all use different criteria to judge whether a technique is prudent, curious, natural, deceptive, or sinful.

This diversity makes divination one of the portal's major hub concepts. It is where one sees most clearly that the history of medieval magic is not only about conjurations and grimoires, but also about the desire to know what should remain uncertain: the future, hidden motives, unseen causes, divine favor, bodily prognosis, lost objects, or secret conditions of the world. Divination is therefore tied to epistemic desire as much as to any one ritual form.

Named Arts and Local Contexts

Different divinatory arts had different social and intellectual lives. Geomancy could be copied as a technical art with rules and figures; chiromancy might circulate as bodily reading; hydromancy and sortilege often appeared in lists of forbidden practices; astrological judgment could be defended as mathematically grounded while remaining suspect in some applications. This variety is why the portal links divination outward to narrower entries rather than letting the category absorb them. Each art has its own evidentiary profile, textual environment, and history of condemnation or defense.

The category is also shaped by genre. Manuals may teach techniques; scholastic discussions debate causation and licitness; confessional and pastoral literature focus on suspect consultation; chronicles or miracle collections preserve reports of outcomes; and legal texts flatten several practices into one accusatory field. Divination thus becomes legible only when it is tracked across genres rather than extracted from them.

Scholarly Significance

As a modern category, divination is useful precisely because it allows comparison without pretending that comparison erases difference. Catherine Rider and Jean-Patrice Boudet are especially important here because they show how medieval divinatory techniques were distributed across medicine, theology, prediction, natural inquiry, and occult speculation. The category is strongest when it functions as a hub that clarifies relation, not as a blanket term that removes texture.

Divination also matters because it is one of the best places to study the boundary between natural and illicit knowledge. A prognostic procedure may be treated as medicine, astronomy, prudence, curiosity, or superstition depending on its claims and context. That instability makes divination central to the history of classification. The issue is not only what people did, but how authorities sorted kinds of knowing and kinds of risk.

Knowledge, Uncertainty, and Control

At a deeper level, divination reveals a persistent medieval concern with the management of uncertainty. Prognostic arts promise orientation in situations where ordinary judgment seems inadequate: illness, travel, politics, weather, fertility, litigation, or danger. This promise can make divination look intellectually adjacent to accepted disciplines even when moralists condemn it. The category therefore helps explain why suspicion and reliance could coexist. Practices criticized in one register might still answer real needs in another.

The same point helps explain why divination often shades into neighboring domains. Ritual consultation may overlap with prayer, medicine, computation, memory, or spirit work. A hard separation between divination and the rest of medieval learned or practical culture is therefore historically misleading. The boundaries were negotiated, and the negotiation is part of the archive.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Divinatory material often survives in small and mobile units: a brief section in a larger compilation, a chapter in a mirror-for-princes, a pastoral list, a notebook appendix, or a compact art in a technical miscellany. This archival pattern means divination is not simply a genre but a recurrent function within many genres. The portal uses it as a relational category tied to manuscripts, codicological settings, and local contexts of use.

The category's variant forms are also why it cannot be reduced to one master narrative. Dream books, lots, bodily arts, astral judgments, and spirit consultations do not share one logic, even when later authorities condemn them together. Good historical use of the term keeps these internal differences visible.

Related Concepts

Divination belongs naturally with geomancy, chiromancy, hydromancy, and sortilege as narrower arts. It also intersects with superstitio where pastoral and theological writers classify unlawful consultation, and with necromancy where hidden knowledge is sought through spirits rather than through natural or symbolic signs. Its relationship to celestial influence is especially important wherever astrology or astral theory underwrites prediction.

Literature

Boudet, Jean-Patrice. Studies on learned divination and medieval occult sciences.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on divination, medicine, and pastoral classification in the medieval West.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Weill-Parot, Nicolas. Studies on astrology and the classification of occult knowledge.

Geomancy is an Actor Term for a divinatory art in which patterned combinations of points or marks are generated, arranged, and interpreted to produce judgments about hidden matters. It became one of the most widely discussed and transmitted specific arts of divination in the medieval West. For MedievalMagicDB it matters because it is both a sharply defined technical practice and a recurring touchstone in pastoral, theological, and learned classifications of suspect knowledge.

Historical Usage

Medieval sources typically treat geomancy as one art among others rather than as a synonym for divination in general. Manuals explain figures and judgments, while theological and pastoral texts classify the practice through broader concerns about hidden knowledge, stars, chance, and illicit inquiry. The art's relative technical clarity makes it especially valuable to historians. Unlike very broad labels such as superstition, geomancy names a specific procedure with its own textual traditions and interpretive vocabulary.

Scholarly Significance

Geomancy matters because it shows how divinatory practices could be at once technical, textual, and suspect. It appears in learned settings, in compilations alongside astrology and chiromancy, and in condemnatory literature that treats it as part of a wider field of forbidden arts. As a result it is one of the best narrower entries through which to understand the larger category of divination. It is also useful for reminding the reader that medieval magical and divinatory history is not only about spirit conjuration or grimoires.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The art circulated in Latin and vernacular forms and could appear as a self-contained manual or as one part of a larger compendium of practical knowledge. Its diagrams, named figures, and interpretive structures made it portable across manuscripts. That portability also exposed it to repeated moral scrutiny. Geomancy is therefore a case where textual transmission and condemnation move in tandem.

Related Concepts

Geomancy belongs first of all with divination, of which it is a narrower and more concrete example. It also sits beside chiromancy and sortilege as related arts of hidden judgment, and it may enter discussions of superstitio when pastoral writers classify it as illicit dependence.

Literature

Boudet, Jean-Patrice. Studies on learned divination and medieval occult sciences.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on divination and pastoral classification.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.
Studies on medieval geomantic manuals and manuscript transmission.

db_hits.text:
Chiromancy is an Actor Term for palmistry or hand-reading, a divinatory practice treated in medieval manuals and frequently named in broader condemnations of forbidden arts. The term is historically specific enough to resist flattening into "divination" in general, yet common enough in learned and pastoral classifications that it serves as an important test case for how particular arts are gathered into larger moral and epistemic debates.

Historical Usage

Medieval texts typically list chiromancy among the arts that claim to infer hidden character, fate, or future outcomes from bodily signs. Its interest lies precisely in that relation between observation and prediction. Unlike spirit conjuration, chiromancy can appear close to natural sign-reading, yet authorities often classify it as suspect because it overreaches the legitimate interpretation of nature. This makes it a revealing middle case between natural knowledge and illicit divination.

Scholarly Significance

Chiromancy matters because it shows that medieval divinatory history is not reducible to either astrology or necromancy. It represents a bodily and semiotic art whose status depends heavily on context and classification. Historiographically, it helps explain why medieval authorities often worried about unauthorized prediction even where demonic pact or explicit ritual did not seem central.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The art circulated in manuals, compendia, and lists of forbidden knowledge. It could appear alongside geomancy, physiognomy, and sortilege in larger surveys of divinatory practice. This manuscript and classificatory mobility is one reason chiromancy deserves its own portal entry. Its importance lies not only in technique but in how often it is made to stand for the problem of reading signs too confidently.

Related Concepts

Chiromancy belongs naturally with divination and geomancy as one narrower predictive art among others. It also intersects with Secret of Secrets and physiognomic traditions where bodily interpretation and elite knowledge can overlap with suspect prediction.

Literature

Boudet, Jean-Patrice. Studies on learned divination and occult sciences.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on divination and pastoral classification.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.
Studies on medieval manuals of chiromancy and related bodily arts.

Charms is an Analyst Term for spoken, written, or performed formulae used for healing, protection, coercion, discovery, or divination. The category is indispensable, but it is also slippery. Medieval actors might describe the same material as prayer, blessing, remedy, superstition, or illicit rite depending on setting and authority. The portal therefore uses "charms" as a modern grouping that must remain tethered to genre, manuscript context, and evidentiary register.

Historical Usage

In medieval manuscripts and pastoral sources, what modern scholars call charms appears in many forms: verbal healing sequences, written formulae carried on the body, prayers adapted to practical ends, liturgical fragments reused for protection, and short adjurations aimed at animals, weather, illness, or uncertainty. The historical category is plural by design. Some texts are clearly devotional, some clearly suspect, and many occupy an unstable ground between remedy and ritual dependence.

This variability is historically productive. It reveals that magical classification often began not with spectacular rites but with ordinary practical acts whose licitness could be debated. A charm might look like medicine in one setting, false religion in another, and routine care in a third.

Scholarly Significance

Work by Lea Olsan, Don Skemer, Catherine Rider, and Sophie Page has made charms central to the study of medieval ritual life. The category matters because it reveals how often practical textuality operated below the level of major grimoires and demonological treatises. A short formula may tell us as much about lived religion, manuscript use, and pastoral concern as a much larger learned ritual book.

Charms also matter because they resist clear partition. They move among medicine, devotion, household practice, and magical suspicion. Historiographically, they are one of the best archives for studying everyday ambiguity rather than exceptional transgression.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Charms survive in Latin and vernacular forms, in manuscripts large and small, and often in highly mobile textual units. A charm can be copied into a medical collection, a devotional miscellany, a household notebook, or an amulet. This mobility complicates attempts to define the genre too rigidly. Spoken performance, written portability, and codicological embedding all matter. The charm is often less a "book" than a reusable textual fragment.

Related Concepts

Charms belong closely with amulets, since written and wearable protective forms often overlap. They also intersect with confession, because pastoral texts frequently judged the licitness of such practices, and with superstitio, which often supplied the language of condemnation. Their more suspect forms may shade toward maleficium or divinatory practice, but those relations must be argued case by case.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Amulets is an Analyst Term for objects worn, carried, displayed, or deposited for protection, healing, influence, or identification with particular sacred or occult powers. The term is useful across medieval scholarship, but it gathers together historically diverse objects: inscribed slips, folded texts, medals, images, herbs, stones, and composite devices. In MedievalMagicDB it functions as a bridge concept linking written protection, material religion, charms, and occult object theory.

Historical Usage

Medieval actors did not always call an object an "amulet" in any uniform sense. They might describe a written protection, a sign, a phylactery, a blessed object, a medical item, or an image prepared for a particular effect. This plurality matters because it prevents the modern category from becoming too smooth. Some objects now treated as amulets participated in ordinary devotional practice; others were condemned as superstitious or illicit; still others belonged to medicine, astral image theory, or written apotropaic traditions. The historical field is therefore one of overlap rather than purity.

Scholarly Significance

Amulets matter because they reveal how portable and material medieval ritual knowledge could be. Don Skemer's work on written amulets is especially important here, as are studies linking protective objects to manuscript culture and devotional practice. The category forces scholars to move beyond a text-only understanding of magic while still remaining alert to the fact that many amulets are themselves textual. They are often books in miniature, folded or worn rather than shelved.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The amulet exists in multiple media: parchment, metal, cloth, stone, image, and writing. This variety makes transmission partly codicological and partly material. Instructions for amulets may appear in manuscripts of charms, medical recipes, ritual compilations, or astral texts, while the surviving objects themselves preserve another layer of evidence. The category therefore depends on both textual and non-textual archives. It also overlaps with astral and talismanic traditions without becoming identical to them.

Related Concepts

Amulets belong closely with charms, especially where the protective force is carried by written words. They also intersect with talismans, though the latter usually foreground more explicitly theorized astral or image-based causation. Finally, they are tied to superstitio and confession because clerical authorities often judged such objects through pastoral and theological frameworks.

Literature

Olsan, Lea T. Studies on written and verbal protective practices.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on healing, fertility, and practical ritual in medieval contexts.
Page, Sophie. Studies on manuscripts, protection, and occult textuality.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.

db_hits.text:
Talismans is a Hybrid category in medieval magic scholarship. Modern historians use the term broadly for crafted objects intended to mediate celestial, spiritual, or symbolic power, while medieval texts more often speak of images, figures, characters, or seals in more specific ways. The category is indispensable for understanding astral image theory and the Arabic-Latin occult sciences, but it must be handled carefully because "talisman" can become too smooth a label for a field whose historical vocabularies were much rougher.

Historical Usage

Medieval Latin materials associated with talismanic practice often concern images made under specific celestial conditions, inscribed objects, prepared materials, and theories of natural or astral causation. Yet the texts themselves do not always use one equivalent term consistently. Instead they describe what the object is, how it is related to a planet or sign, or what sort of influence it mediates. This is especially visible in the Latin reception of Arabic image magic, where translation and adaptation create a layered vocabulary.

The category therefore exists partly because modern scholarship needs a comparative word for a recurring phenomenon. Used well, it clarifies; used badly, it erases the distinctions medieval authors actually made among images, seals, characters, and prepared objects.

Scholarly Significance

Modern scholarship relies on "talismans" because it provides a bridge among image-making traditions, astral theory, and manuscript evidence. Nicolas Weill-Parot and Jean-Patrice Boudet have shown how important it is to distinguish different theories of image efficacy rather than treating every image-object as the same phenomenon. The category is strongest when it remains analytic and relational.

Talismans also matter because they keep the materiality of ritual practice in view. Many histories of magic drift toward names, diagrams, and texts; talismanic traditions insist that matter, craft, and cosmic timing are equally central.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The field is deeply shaped by Arabic-Latin transmission. Concepts translated from Arabic occult science entered Latin through works such as the Picatrix, attributed texts of Thabit ibn Qurra, and other astral-image materials. Across this movement, terminology shifted. Image, seal, figure, and talismanic object do not always overlap perfectly. The manuscript evidence also reveals recombination: image recipes, theoretical passages, and practical notes could be excerpted into larger compilations, making the talismanic tradition a network rather than a single corpus.

Related Concepts

Talismans are especially close to astral image magic and celestial influence, since the category often depends on planetary causation. They also intersect with amulets, though the latter can extend more broadly into protective and devotional object culture. Finally, the category belongs with suffumigation and characters and seals, both of which recur in image-oriented textual traditions.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Suffumigation is an Actor Term for ritual fumigation with incense, herbs, resins, or other substances, often coordinated with purification, planetary timing, spirit address, or image consecration. It is one of the most technically recurrent practices in medieval ritual and astral texts. For MedievalMagicDB it matters because it sits at the intersection of material procedure, sensory environment, and theories of efficacy.

Historical Usage

In medieval texts suffumigation may appear as preparation, offering, environmental conditioning, or an active part of a ritual operation. It is especially common in astral and image-oriented traditions, where materials and times are coordinated, but it also appears in spirit-oriented and protective contexts. The term matters historically because it shows how much learned ritual depended on the management of substances and atmosphere, not just on words or diagrams.

Scholarly Significance

Suffumigation is important because it highlights the materiality of medieval ritual books. A manuscript can preserve names and figures, but it can also preserve expectations about smell, smoke, ingredients, and embodied preparation. This makes the practice useful for historians interested in ritual ecology and not only textual form. It also helps connect astral image theory with more overtly conjurational traditions.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The practice appears across multiple textual families, including the Picatrix, image-magic treatises, and other ritual books. Its recurrence does not imply uniform meaning. In some texts it is framed cosmologically, in others purificatory or coercive. The term therefore names a family of ritual uses rather than a single stable operation. That variability is part of its scholarly interest.

Related Concepts

Suffumigation is closely linked to talismans and astral image magic, where substances often mediate celestial or imagistic efficacy. It also intersects with ritual purity and angelic invocation where preparation and spiritual relation matter.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Voces Magicae is an Analyst Term for nonordinary ritual words, names, and syllables whose efficacy is attached to sound, secrecy, antiquity, divine authority, or angelic transmission. The expression is modern and comparative, but it captures an important historical phenomenon: medieval ritual texts often preserve verbal units that appear strange, foreign, opaque, or deliberately set apart from ordinary language.

Historical Usage

Medieval actors did not generally use the exact phrase voces magicae as a self-description. Instead they encountered sequences of names, syllables, and words in ritual books, notory prayers, angelic or divine invocations, and protective writing. Some were explained as sacred names; some remained opaque; some drew authority precisely from their foreignness or secrecy. The historical point is that unusual words could function as ritual technologies in their own right.

Scholarly Significance

The category matters because it draws attention to the sonic and textual dimensions of medieval ritual. A magical book is not only diagrams, substances, or timings; it is also a repository of words whose power may depend on pronunciation, mystery, and correct transmission. The term is therefore especially useful for linking learned ritual texts with broader histories of sacred language and verbal efficacy.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Such words survive across multiple textual environments: Ars Notoria, angelic invocations, spirit conjurations, protective texts, and revelatory books. Their forms vary widely, and scribal corruption or adaptation is often part of the story. This makes them a particularly rich topic for manuscript study, because their instability is historically meaningful rather than accidental noise.

Related Concepts

Voces magicae belongs closely with secret names, angelic invocation, and notae, since all concern ritual forms whose efficacy depends on exact transmission. It also intersects with learned magic where verbal secrecy and textual authority become central.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Ritual Purity is a Hybrid category for requirements of confession, fasting, abstinence, cleanliness, prayer, moral preparation, and bodily or spiritual discipline that structure many medieval ritual texts. It is crucial for MedievalMagicDB because it shows that the learned magical archive often framed itself through ethical and devotional preparation rather than through sheer technique. Purity is one of the clearest points where ritual magic and religious practice approach each other without becoming identical.

Historical Usage

In medieval ritual books, requirements of purity can include sacramental confession, sexual abstinence, fasting, prayer, bodily cleanliness, or other forms of moral and liturgical preparation. These requirements appear especially strongly in traditions of angelic invocation, notory prayer, and elevated knowledge, though they are not limited to those fields. Their historical significance lies in what they reveal about ritual self-understanding. Many texts did not imagine efficacy as merely technical; they imagined it as dependent on disciplined relation to the sacred.

Scholarly Significance

Ritual purity matters because it complicates any simple opposition between devotion and magic. Claire Fanger's work on John of Morigny and learned ritual has made this point especially vivid, while Page, Klaassen, and others have shown how such requirements appear across manuscript traditions. The category is therefore central to the historiography of learned magic, particularly where modern readers are tempted to isolate procedures from their moral and liturgical framing.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Purity requirements survive in prayers, rubrics, narrative frames, and lists of preparation. They can be modest or extensive, and their exact meaning changes across texts. In some books they emphasize spiritual elevation; in others they regulate dangerous interaction with spirits or names. The concept thus names a family of ritual expectations rather than one fixed medieval doctrine.

Related Concepts

Ritual purity belongs closely with confession and angelic invocation, both of which frequently structure ritual preparation. It also intersects with notae and learned magic, since the most textually elaborate rituals are often also the most morally demanding in self-presentation.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Experimental Science is an Analyst Term whose importance in this portal is chiefly historiographical. It is strongly associated with large modern syntheses, above all Lynn Thorndike's A History of Magic and Experimental Science, in which magic and experiment appear as intertwined attempts to understand and manipulate nature. Medieval actors did not possess a single stable category equivalent to the phrase, and modern historians use it cautiously because it can blur crucial differences among natural philosophy, practical testing, occult causality, and illicit arts.

Historical Usage

There is no exact medieval actor term corresponding to experimental science as one unified field. Medieval writers spoke instead of experimentum, experience, mathematical arts, natural philosophy, properties, causes, and specific arts or operations. Figures such as Roger Bacon, Albertus Magnus, al-Kindi, Michael Scot, and later scholastic compilers become relevant here because they wrote about disciplined inquiry into causes, effects, rays, celestial influence, or hidden structures of nature. Those inquiries often touch the same problems modern historians place near magic.

That overlap is historically important, but it should not be overstated. Medieval authors did not simply think "science and magic are the same." They argued over what counted as lawful inquiry, what relied on occult properties, what could be demonstrated from nature, and what risked illicit dependence on stars, spirits, or unknown powers. Experimental science is therefore most useful as a label for an unstable zone of inquiry rather than as a triumphalist anticipation of modern laboratory science.

Thorndike and the Historiography of Continuity

The phrase matters above all because of the historiographical problem it names. Thorndike's monumental history insisted that medieval and early modern inquiry could not be narrated cleanly as the gradual shedding of magic in favor of science. Instead, attempts to explain unusual effects, verify powers, and manipulate nature often developed in close conversation with astrology, image magic, occult properties, and practical experimenta. Later scholarship has revised Thorndike sharply, but it has not made the underlying problem disappear.

Modern historians now tend to avoid any simple continuity story from magic to science. Yet the category remains useful because it reminds us that medieval natural inquiry did not grow in sterile isolation from occult speculation. The archive itself is messier: recipes, observations, image theories, ray theory, mineral virtues, and astral causation coexist in ways that force historians to think relationally.

Natural Inquiry and Occult Causality

One reason the category remains productive is that it highlights how often medieval inquiry focused on effects that were real or reputed but not fully explicable within ordinary causal language. Hidden virtues of stones, the action of celestial rays, the efficacy of properly timed images, or the transformation of substances all invited analysis that was neither straightforwardly magical nor fully normalized as scholastic natural philosophy. Experimental science, as an analytical label, points to that pressure point where explanation seeks discipline without yet attaining consensus.

Roger Bacon is especially important in this respect because later historians repeatedly recruited him into narratives of experiment, technology, and anti-superstitious critique. Albertus Magnus matters because his discussions of nature, minerals, and hidden properties reveal how inquiry into unusual effects could be methodical without being disenchanted. The category therefore serves less to canonize modern heroes than to illuminate medieval arguments over reliable knowledge.

Scholarly Significance

The category names both a temptation and an insight. The temptation is to read medieval inquiry as proto-modern science steadily purifying itself of magical residue. The insight is that many medieval attempts to explain, test, or manipulate unusual effects did inhabit a shared zone with occult causality, astral influence, and hidden virtues. Thorndike exaggerated some continuities, but he also forced the field to confront the archive that makes such continuities thinkable.

Later scholars have refined the problem by insisting on stronger distinctions among genres, institutions, and explanatory registers. Nicolas Weill-Parot, David Pingree, and others have shown that one must separate astral image theory, mathematical speculation, scholastic natural philosophy, and practical recipe culture with more care than Thorndike sometimes did. Even so, the phrase remains valuable because it names the historiographical work required to keep those distinctions visible without pretending the domains never touched.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Because this is an analyst term, its transmission occurs mainly through modern scholarship rather than through medieval manuscript self-description. Yet it organizes a very real medieval archive: treatises on rays, minerals, celestial influence, experimenta, and natural powers; compilations where practical recipes and theoretical discussions coexist; and later scholarly narratives that sought to bind these materials together. The portal uses the category not to collapse distinctions, but to help the user see why those distinctions became historically urgent.

Its variant forms are especially visible in the way different scholars have populated the label. For some, it stresses observation and practical verification. For others, it names an archive where occult explanation and natural inquiry remain intertwined. The portal follows the latter, more cautious usage: experimental science is a historiographical guide to a mixed field, not a declaration that medieval magic was secretly modern science in disguise.

Related Concepts

Experimental science borders closely on occult properties, natural powers, and celestial influence, all of which concern partially explained efficacy within nature. It also intersects with learned magic where the classificatory problem becomes especially sharp, and with mathematical arts where quantification, observation, and prediction overlap with suspect forms of knowledge.

Literature

Hackett, Jeremiah. Studies on medieval science and scholastic inquiry.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Lindberg, David C. Studies on medieval science and natural philosophy.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Weill-Parot, Nicolas. Studies on the relation of occult science, mathematics, and scholastic classification.

db_hits.text:
Demonology is an Analyst Term for learned discourse about demons: their ontology, powers, limits, deceptive capacities, and relation to human ritual action. It is a crucial category for medieval magic scholarship because many condemnations of magic are really arguments about what demons can accomplish and how they may insinuate themselves into practices of divination, healing, image use, or spirit invocation. Yet demonology should never be allowed to stand in for the whole field of magic. It is one powerful framework among others.

Historical Usage

Medieval actors did not usually gather all relevant materials under a single neutral heading equivalent to the modern word demonology. Rather, discussions of demons appear in theology, biblical exegesis, pastoral literature, scholastic disputation, exorcistic practice, and polemic against suspect arts. This dispersed archive is historically important. It shows that demonological reasoning was not marginal but woven into mainstream religious thought. At the same time, the relation between demons and magic was not uniform. Some texts make demons central to efficacy; others argue that certain effects can be natural or illusory instead.

Scholarly Significance

Demonology matters because it helps historians understand how the category of illicit magic was built. William of Auvergne, Thomas Aquinas, Augustine, and later demonological writers all provided conceptual grammars for reading ritual actions through demonic causation. Modern scholars such as Michael D. Bailey and Richard Kieckhefer have shown that this framework must be studied alongside manuscript evidence rather than accepted as direct description of practice. Demonology is therefore both source and problem: it tells us how authorities thought, and it risks obscuring how practitioners understood themselves.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The field moves across patristic, scholastic, pastoral, and legal traditions. A demonological claim in Augustine does not function exactly like one in a confessor's manual, an exorcistic rite, or a later witchcraft treatise. The category also shifts across vernacularization and institutional use. Medieval demonology is thus better treated as a family of discourses than as a single stable science. The modern term names a real scholarly object, but a composite one.

Related Concepts

Demonology is tightly linked to exorcism, where demonic presence becomes a matter of ritual management. It also intersects with necromancy and demonic pact, though those concepts concern particular forms of suspected or theorized relation to spirits. In histories of accusation it also bears strongly on witchcraft and maleficium.

Literature

Bailey, Michael D. Studies on medieval demonology, religion, and witchcraft.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on theology, pastoral care, and suspect practices.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.

db_hits.text:
Witchcraft is an Analyst Term for a historically contingent cluster of accusations, learned constructions, beliefs, and legal or theological categories that became especially powerful in the later Middle Ages and early modern period. It does not map cleanly onto a single medieval actor term. Medieval writers more often used words such as maleficium, superstitio, sortilegium, and related labels of harm, false religion, or illicit practice. The portal therefore treats witchcraft as a necessary but unstable modern category whose value depends on careful attention to local evidence, chronology, and vocabulary.

Historical Usage

The medieval roots of what modern historians call witchcraft are dispersed across sermons, confessional manuals, miracle stories, canon law, scholastic theology, princely advice, vernacular narrative, and judicial record. A village-level charge of bodily or agricultural harm, a theologian's discussion of demons, and a pastoral warning against suspect cures all contribute different strands to the category. This dispersion matters because it means witchcraft was assembled historically out of partially connected fears and explanatory habits rather than inherited as a single fixed social reality.

Many early and central uses of the evidence revolve around harm. Livestock fail, weather threatens, fertility is disrupted, illness persists, impotence is feared, and someone is said to have caused it. Yet the explanatory language for such harm remains variable. One source may treat the problem as maleficium, another as deception by demons, another as superstitious misuse of sacred words or objects, and still another as social suspicion with minimal theoretical elaboration. The term "witchcraft" is therefore strongest when it is used not as an assumed essence but as a map of converging accusations and interpretations.

By the thirteenth through fifteenth centuries, several developments begin to tighten the field. Scholastic demonology expands the explanatory place of the devil and subordinate spirits. Pastoral programs sharpen concern with illicit rites and suspect consultations. Judicial and inquisitorial procedures provide stronger institutional mechanisms for gathering testimony and codifying suspicion. Vernacular didactic texts and learned polemics alike begin to align harmful action, hidden ritual, perverse intention, and demonic agency more explicitly than before. None of these developments alone "creates" witchcraft, but together they make the category more historically coherent and more socially dangerous.

Analyst Term and Actor Vocabulary

The category is methodologically important because it is one of the clearest places where modern analytical language can obscure historical vocabulary. Medieval sources usually do not say "witchcraft" in the modern historian's broad sense. They name injuries, unlawful observances, charms, prognostications, image rites, and demonic acts more specifically. To write the history of witchcraft well is therefore to preserve the distance between analyst shorthand and actor speech. The portal keeps that distance visible by linking witchcraft outward to narrower entries rather than letting it swallow them.

This actor-analyst tension also helps explain why the category must remain relational. A confessor concerned about a spoken charm, a theologian arguing about diabolic illusion, and a judge pursuing accusations of harmful magic are not simply describing one shared object. They are repositioning overlapping materials within different institutional frames. Witchcraft is the modern historian's name for that unstable but consequential overlap.

Scholarly Significance

Modern scholarship uses witchcraft because some larger term is needed for the convergence of harmful magic, demonological suspicion, social accusation, and legal or moral classification. Michael D. Bailey has been especially important in showing how this convergence belongs to the history of religion, classification, and pastoral reform rather than to a simple story of irrational panic. Richard Kieckhefer's work remains crucial because it helps keep clerical and learned forms of illicit ritual in view even where later witchcraft discourse tries to redraw all magical practice under a single demonological heading.

Catherine Rider, Sophie Page, and contributors to broader synthetic volumes have also helped integrate witchcraft back into the wider ecology of medieval magic. That move matters because the field can otherwise split artificially between elite ritual magic on one side and village accusation on the other. Medieval evidence rarely respects such a clean division. Theories, texts, and accusations moved across social registers in messy ways, and witchcraft is one of the places where that mess becomes historically visible.

Accusation, Practice, and Social Form

Witchcraft should not be reduced either to actual ritual practice or to fantasy imposed from above. Medieval evidence frequently contains both practice claims and accusatory projection, and the relative balance changes by genre. Some records preserve only polemical attribution. Others point to routines of healing, cursing, protective speech, or consultation that may have been locally meaningful apart from learned demonology. Historians therefore have to distinguish among performed action, narrated suspicion, legal scripting, and theological interpretation.

This distinction is especially important for the medieval period, where later early modern stereotypes can easily distort the archive. The sabbath complex, systematic confessions of pact, and strong gendered consolidation associated with later witch persecutions have medieval antecedents, but they should not be retrojected wholesale into earlier centuries. The portal's entry architecture accordingly treats witchcraft as a moving category with a long prehistory rather than as a fully formed medieval constant.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Witchcraft is shaped not by one grimoire or one legal code but by the circulation of ideas across theology, canon law, pastoral practice, miracle narratives, exempla, demonological speculation, and vernacular teaching. In some texts, harmful action is central and demonic theory secondary. In others, the reverse is true. Some sources foreground women; others worry more about clerics, diviners, healers, or household specialists. The category's variation is therefore part of its history, not an imperfection to be cleaned away.

The relation between witchcraft and learned magic is likewise unstable. Some authors carefully distinguish clerical ritual books from popular harmful practices. Others absorb both into a single logic of illicit commerce with spirits. This instability is one reason the portal treats witchcraft as a hub entry: it forces the user to browse among accusation, ritual technique, moral theology, and institutional classification rather than settling too quickly on one explanatory model.

Related Concepts

Witchcraft overlaps most obviously with maleficium, though the latter names harm more precisely and historically. It also intersects with demonology and demonic pact, which often provide the explanatory framework for accusations. Finally, it bears on charms, divination, and confession, where ordinary, protective, or prognostic acts may be redrawn as suspect under pastoral or polemical pressure.

Literature

Bailey, Michael D. Magic and Superstition in Europe: A Concise History from Antiquity to the Present. Lanham: Rowman & Littlefield, 2007.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 1998.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on pastoral and legal classification in the medieval West.

db_hits.text:
Exorcism is a Hybrid category because it names both authorized Christian rites for expelling or restraining spirits and adjacent ritual forms that could blur into illicit command, adjuration, or coercive spirit management. For MedievalMagicDB it is one of the most revealing boundary concepts in the corpus. Exorcism forces the reader to confront how close liturgical, pastoral, and magical forms could appear in language and gesture even when ecclesiastical legitimacy sharply distinguished them.

Historical Usage

In medieval usage exorcism belongs first to the sphere of authorized Christian ritual. It appears in liturgical books, pastoral practice, and sacramental or quasi-sacramental settings concerned with demonic disturbance, purification, and protection. Yet the same verbal technologies of adjuration and command could also appear in suspect rites and magical manuscripts. This does not mean the two spheres are identical. It means that historians must pay careful attention to institutional authorization, intention, textual frame, and manuscript setting.

Scholarly Significance

Exorcism matters because it shows why the history of medieval magic cannot be reduced to a simple binary between orthodoxy and deviance. Authorized ritual and illicit spirit work may share forms of address while differing radically in status and interpretation. Scholars such as Florence Chave-Mahir, Claire Fanger, and Richard Kieckhefer have helped make this borderland visible. The category is especially valuable when analyzing angelic and demonic ritual texts that borrow from or imitate authoritative liturgical styles.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Exorcistic materials circulate through service books, pastoral manuals, narrative accounts, and magical compilations. This variety means that the category has no single archive. Some exorcistic language is stable and official; some is mobile and excerpted; some is recontextualized into ritual handbooks that test the boundary of legitimacy. The study of exorcism therefore requires both liturgical history and manuscript comparison.

Related Concepts

Exorcism belongs naturally with demonology, since theories of spirit action shape ritual response. It also intersects with angelic invocation and ritual purity, because authorized and suspect rites alike often stress preparation and right relation to spiritual power. In pastoral settings it is also close to confession.

Literature

db_hits.text:
Hydromancy is an Actor Term for divination by water, frequently named in medieval taxonomies of forbidden or suspect arts. It is historically important not because it dominates the surviving manuscript archive, but because it repeatedly appears in lists and classifications that map the diversity of divinatory practices. In the portal it helps demonstrate how broad and differentiated the medieval vocabulary of prediction could be.

Historical Usage

Medieval writers often mention hydromancy alongside geomancy, chiromancy, pyromancy, and sortilege. These grouped arts were frequently treated as examples of illicit efforts to uncover hidden truths through unstable media. Water's reflective, moving, and receptive qualities made it a potent medium for such interpretive practice. The category therefore belongs strongly to the history of divinatory classification.

Scholarly Significance

Hydromancy matters because it shows that medieval anti-divinatory discourse regularly worked by enumeration. Even where a particular art is thinly documented in surviving manuals, its repeated appearance in taxonomic lists tells historians something about how the field of forbidden knowledge was imagined. Hydromancy is thus valuable as an entry in its own right and as part of a larger classificatory pattern.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The practice survives more strongly as a named category in condemnatory and encyclopedic listings than as a single famous text family. That makes it a good example of an art whose historical weight comes partly from classification rather than from abundant dedicated manuals. Its place in the portal is therefore relational and comparative.

Related Concepts

Hydromancy belongs with divination, geomancy, and pyromancy as part of a recurring medieval family of named arts. It also intersects with superstitio where authorities classify these practices as improper dependence on uncertain signs.

Literature

Boudet, Jean-Patrice. Studies on learned divination and occult sciences.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on pastoral and theological classification of divinatory arts.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.
Studies on medieval taxonomies of divination and forbidden arts.

db_hits.text:
Notae is an Actor Term for the visual signs or figures most closely associated with the Ars Notoria tradition and with related modes of ritual learning. The term belongs to the medieval textual world itself rather than being a purely modern analytical label. It names diagrammatic forms that are integral to the ritual logic of certain manuscripts, not mere illustration. For that reason notae are central to any historically serious account of ritualized learning, memory, and angelic mediation in the later Middle Ages.

Historical Usage

In manuscript witnesses of the Ars Notoria and related texts, notae appear as structured visual signs linked to prayers, recitations, and ordered sequences of preparation. Medieval users did not treat them as decorative embellishment. They were part of the mechanism by which learning, understanding, and divine assistance were supposed to be pursued. The exact form and arrangement of the notae vary across manuscripts, which makes codicology and visual transmission crucial.

This historical embeddedness matters because it prevents the term from being reduced to general talk of magical diagrams. Notae belong to a specific ritual-learning environment and carry that environment with them even when reproduced or excerpted.

Scholarly Significance

Modern scholars such as Claire Fanger, Richard Kieckhefer, Frank Klaassen, and Robert Mathiesen have emphasized that the visual dimension of ritual learning cannot be reduced to words alone. Notae concentrate several key problems at once: the relation between prayer and image, the role of discipline in acquiring knowledge, and the instability of ritual books across transmission. They also resist smooth translation into purely verbal summary, which is one reason they are such an important test of good scholarly method.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The notae differ from manuscript to manuscript in form, arrangement, and accompanying explanation. Some are transmitted with more elaborate paratexts, while others are embedded in more compact ritual sequences. This variation means that the term names a family of visual instruments rather than one invariant set of figures. The study of notae is accordingly bound up with the history of editions, reproductions, and modern attempts to make these diagrams legible in print.

Because of that variability, notae are best studied as manuscript events rather than abstract symbols detached from copying practice. The portal treats them as one of the clearest cases where codicology and ritual interpretation must remain tightly linked.

Related Concepts

Notae are inseparable from Ars Notoria, which gives them their primary manuscript environment. They also belong with ritual diagrams and learned magic, though those broader categories should never erase the specifically medieval status of the term. Their devotional framing often ties them to angelic invocation and ritual purity.

Literature

Fanger, Claire. Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices, Thirteenth to Sixteenth Centuries. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2012.
Klaassen, Frank. The Transformations of Magic: Illicit Learned Magic in the Later Middle Ages and Renaissance. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2013.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 1998.
Mathiesen, Robert. Studies on the Ars Notoria and related ritual-learning traditions.
Skinner, Stephen, and Daniel Clark. Ars Notoria: The Grimoire of Rapid Learning by Magic with the Golden Flowers of Apollonius of Tyana. Modern edition and study.
